abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have catalyzed a revolution in computer vision, enabling unprecedented advancements across diverse tasks such as image classification, object detection, and video prediction. By leveraging autonomous feature learning and hierarchical expression, CNNs have largely superseded traditional machine learning approaches that rely on manual feature engineering. This review provides a comprehensive exploration of the CNN landscape, beginning with a fundamental analysis of core components, including convolutional and pooling layers, activation functions, batch normalization, dropout, and fully connected architectures. We examine the evolution of seminal models from early frameworks like AlexNet and VGG to sophisticated modern architectures such as ResNet, GoogLeNet, and the attention-based SENet, while also considering the efficiency of lightweight models like MobileNet for resource-constrained environments. Furthermore, we categorize and evaluate object detection methodologies, distinguishing between two-stage and one-stage detectors, and discuss the emerging frontiers of spatio-temporal modeling for video prediction. By synthesizing current research trends, this paper identifies persistent challenges—such as the demand for extensive labeled data, computational complexity, and the need for improved model interpretability—and outlines future research directions to guide the development of next-generation visual intelligence systems.
